ThinkPHP Day 7: Where Does Everything Live? A Tour of the Directory Structure
We've already got ThinkPHP 5.1 installed. But instead of diving straight into building things, let's pump the brakes for a second.

Here's the thing — if you don't understand the directory structure, you'll constantly be lost. You won't know where things live, and you definitely won't know where you're supposed to put your own code. So this chapter is all about getting comfortable with how ThinkPHP lays out its folders.
First, let's take a look at the directory structure of the ThinkPHP 5.1 we just installed:
I'm going to list out the main folders here. These are the core files and directories, so please make a mental note of them:
— the entry file for the command line
This is where all of ThinkPHP's main core functionality lives. Generally speaking, you won't touch this.
In the early days ThinkPHP put the site's entry file in the root directory, but from 5.1 onward it moved into
folder — where your routes go (yeah, I know, shocking)
A lot of your route config goes here, so usually there's just a single file in it:
folder — where a ton of your settings live
It contains a bunch of important config files, like
folder — where your controllers, models, views, etc. go
We'll be doing most of our setup and tweaking in here later on.

One more thing: ThinkPHP decides its URLs mainly through routes. If you haven't registered any routes, it falls back to the original default-style access, like the official manual describes:
Browsing things the original way is honestly kind of a pain (sweat).

We've already got ThinkPHP 5.1 installed, but instead of jumping straight into building stuff, let's slow down for a second. If you don't understand the directory structure, you'll have no idea where things are — or worse, where they're supposed to go. So in this chapter we'll walk through ThinkPHP's folder layout, look at the entry files, and even cover how to rename the core folder (and why I'd really rather you didn't).
